speaker: interviewer
text: You were just telling me how now you had to inspect the rifles and the uniforms and this anti-Semite came. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wouldn't remember now. I wouldn't know his name. Anyway, when he came, he came in like other soldiers came in to me. And I had to write it out. And later I go into the captain's office and he had to sign it, so he can go out for the day, you know, like a Sunday, Saturday, mostly a Sunday, go out to the city, you know, like other soldiers used to do. Sometimes we used to go also a Sunday, like with the-- on the horses through the whole city of Przemysl to ride around all the streets, you know, not to parade, just to go with the horses around. And people were coming out, looking, you know. It was really nice, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, when he came in and he saw me, he said, what are you doing here? I said, what are you doing here? What are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He say-- I say, this is my office, because they-- they sent him over. He was in another-- in another battalion. But later, they sent him over. They keep changing around, you know, some soldiers. So they changed him to my-- to my battalion. But this is the first time he came. He wanted to go to the city to meet somebody, a friend or whatever. So I said, this is my office and my right. He say, I need to go to the city. Sunday, I have a friend or somebody, a cousin, came over. I say, you remember what you told me, [NON-ENGLISH]. Jew, so and so. I said, I told you, you're going to clean my boots, I'll give you-- I'll give you the referral to go to the city. He says, yes. I have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took off my boots and I gave it to him. And he went on the side and I opened up the magazine to go and to take and to clean all my shoes. That's what he did. I said, next time don't say [NON-ENGLISH], you because you don't know who somebody is. I'm a Jew, but I'm doing better than you and other 100 Polacks like you, anti-Semites.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not afraid. I was really-- I had a paper from my captain. I had a paper. When I go to the city, I had-- my uniform was made to order, like all for the officers, just I didn't have nothing here. I was a soldier, only a soldier. But I had everything. This soldier is allowed [NON-ENGLISH]-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH] with the horses-- is allowed to wear [INAUDIBLE], because you have to have it, otherwise right-- the military police, the special military police, they were catching soldiers who are not dressed right or whatever, they were doing something wrong, you know, and arrest them, send them back to the [INAUDIBLE], but with an officer [INAUDIBLE] put them in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had everything written down. I'm allowed to wear this uniform. I'm allowed to wear-- even we used to have to go in the city-- have to go, we have to have the sabers. We used to have the sabers on the side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it happened-- we did not used to do it from the beginning. It happened that in the city, one butcher somehow-- I don't know where. He went to buy salami or whatever to a butcher and he got an argument with the butcher in the store in Przemysl and he beat him up. The butcher beat him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, when he came back, he came already to the higher-- higher offices and say, such things shouldn't happen. A Polish soldier shouldn't be beat up by somebody, a civilian. This is unbelievable. So everybody came-- nobody goes to the city without a saber. You have to have it that you can protect yourself. So anyway, but I didn't want to go. I had cousins, nice cousins, girls. I used to go out. And this was bothering me, so I told my captain, I told him, can I have a lady's-- a lady's revolver, like office-- like the officer? He say, sure, why not? And [INAUDIBLE] write in the paper, in my-- in my report. So I was really good up, and I came to visit my-- and my cousin has a liquor store where all my-- I came once to my cousin, [INAUDIBLE], to the liquor store. And I came there and I didn't see in the corner-- he had a big place-- there were all my officers sitting there and drinking. And I came over. My cousin came out from behind the counter. He came in and grabbed me. We kissed each other. And my cousin spotted the feather that I'm from his-- from their regiment, you know? So they-- they came out and they say [NON-ENGLISH]. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was without an arm. He was in the First World War. That was-- cut off his arm. He had only one arm he had.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I see my-- my [INAUDIBLE], so right away I salute. My-- the captain-- the captain and other officers were there, lieutenants. He said, this is my cousin. This is my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. This is your officers? Everybody, the whole table, drink for free, drink for free. So I had very good in the Polish Army. I didn't have no [INAUDIBLE], nothing. Nothing. I loved it. What was the name of that captain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember no names. Even the-- even the-- the major from the polk, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews were in your division?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had-- my division was maybe five. No, there were more than five. But some of them didn't go with me. Oh, there were about 15, 20 in the division. But in my place was six. No, five. Four and I was the fifth in my division, because it happened later when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what division that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the-- we just did the 22nd Division from the light artillery. That's all. So when the war broke out, they didn't send me to the front, because all the German Messerschmidts were flying the first day over our polk. We were with the-- went out everybody with machine guns and shooting. The bullets came down all flat. They didn't touch, because the Messerschmidt, they were one of the best Czechoslovakian planes. That's why Hitler took over Czechoslovakia, Austria and-- Austria took over because, you know, he was from Austria, Hitler. But Czechoslovakia took over on account of their factor, they had the best jets. Those years nobody had this kind of-- like the Messerschmidts. They called them the Messerschmidts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were going and nobody-- even with cannons, you can do nothing, you know. And Poland didn't have too much what to shoot, really poor. But in three weeks, they took it over. So like-- Could you describe where you were geographically on the first of September, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. Where? In the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Przemysl. This was in Przemysl. Oh, we didn't move out. Sure, before we went on maneuvers far away. But this is-- that-- that time we were all [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was putting to the main magazine, the head of magazine. There was three divisions. In a polk was three divisions. I was putting-- in that division, I was putting in to take over the head of the magazine to-- and they're going to take reserves.

speaker: interviewee
text: First they took them and send them to Gdansk, where the front was. Soon they came in with the-- with the trains. They came in to Gdansk. They bombarded all the trains. They all got killed, the first squadron what they sent. Now, the second, we start-- they start bringing in they're call the [NON-ENGLISH], the main office. They start bringing in soldiers reserve. Now, the reserve has to be dressed in uniforms. I was the head of the uniform department to give it out for all them. Then there were for the Jews with me. Oh, there were a lot of people. But I had, you know-- because I was so good, you know. Once it happens also, but I didn't say. It happen when I had the uniform magazine, everything there was inspections coming. Once in three months, once in four months came an inspection from the high rank. And they came in, inspect how the magazines look and what-- you have to have everything bundled up in 10. And I also make signs all around. My magazine was up to date. And then the major came in to inspect. My captain went with him and opened up the doors. And he came in and I was there. And so I went over and I saluted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he stopped. He say, who is-- who is doing this drugstore? This looks to me like a drugstore, not a-- not a uniform magazine. So my captain say, Leon Scher. [POLISH]. They were called in Polish [POLISH]. So he said, yes. I never see something like that. I had signs on each bundle, shirts and undershirts and shoes and uniform clothes, jackets. And everything was lined up just to come check whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's the whole thing. This is so much, so much, so much, so much. So he said-- so he gave me two weeks vacation. So that's why it was written, because I'm-- I'm keeping a good job, so they sent me-- when the war broke out, they sent me to-- to put uniforms on their reserves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sent out about three reserves, three-- three transferred. But then, when the Germans came over [INAUDIBLE] from Przemysl, like 50 miles away, so we pack up. Everybody went away. The major, the big shot went away. There only was a [POLISH] from one-- from one-- from one group, about six officers, and then me. I used to also-- so they used to-- what did they do? Let me see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. So we start packing up. They say, let's pack up whatever we can. We had about 15 wagons, all military wagons with horses. And everything was put in, whatever we can take for the soldiers, rifles and machine guns and everything, and also the safe from the-- from the army, where they were given out every week. You know, every week they'd give out for the soldiers, you know, so much and so much money, zloty, two zlotys, whatever. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so we start packing up. And we are ready. We go. We want to go through Poland, [POLISH] where all the fires were burning. You know, there's [INAUDIBLE]. There's gasoline, gasoline-- magazines, everything was burning. And we went to the site. And they also went with-- with the Messerschmidts starts shooting. A lot of people got killed. But we were going mostly at the night. We were supposed to go to Hungary, go into Hungary with the Polish army, whatever we had left over. Now, we traveled until we came to Stanislowow, Kolomyia. In Kolomyia, we didn't know Germany had a pact with Russia, stop the fighting in between us, with Russia and Germany. We'll give you all the Ukrainian and we'll move out. We'll move out back to the Polish border, like Przemysl was a big water, [INAUDIBLE] they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- is good. We're going to be here. You can go take over all Ukrainia. So that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't know. In the middle of the night, before going in over the border to Hungary, the Russians came over, took us all around, and got us in. Now, what's happened, nobody knowed about that pact. It was a secret pact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the soldiers say later, they took us to a big-- a big farm, like a landowner, big landowner's farm. The landowner was not there no more. He ran away. So we were on this farm. And all the-- we went in with all the wagons, all the [INAUDIBLE]. So what I say, first of all, I took my uniform off, because my uniform was too nice. They would tell me that I rip off everything, whatever I have, and now I'm-- I'm an officer. And I know that they are not going to treat good the officer, so I ripped this off and I put it on the uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to my major and I told him, how about I'll give your uniform to the captain, to the lieutenants. I'll give you uniform, put them on, because it's going to be dangerous for you. This way, they don't know. You know, the officers ran away. They wouldn't know who was an officer, whatever. He say, no. We cannot do that. This is our honor. We cannot put our honor so low that we should put out-- put in soldiers' uniforms. And they didn't do it. What's happened, and came in the morning-- and came in the morning, they lined up all the officers. They lined them up and took them away. Later, we find out that they killed them, that they killed them like they killed the 10,000 Polish officers in Russia. There was going on a fight. The Russians say that the Germans killed 10,000 officers. And-- but now it came out really that the Russians killed 10,000 officers that they took over when they ran away, you know, from Poland. But that's what happened. And now stood the soldiers. They say-- next day, they say you can go to your wives and to your children. You can go home, the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to clarify something, the Russians killed 10,000--

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot the name. In-- in Russia, yeah. They found them in graves-- in graves, big graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Polish officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish officers, yeah. It just came on my mind, remembered the-- the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did you learn this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- this I learned later on-- later on, just, you know, after the war-- after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time, did you know anything about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know what's happened to the officers, because-- so anyway-- and I know where the safe was, but I didn't have the-- the major had the keys from the safe to pay-- to pay out the soldiers. So I called my four friends, the Jewish friends, I called them over. And I say, let's go, fellas. Let's grab the safe. They took-- they took away-- they took away all the officers. I say, let's grab the safe. And I took chisels and hammers and there were shacks, you know, all kind for the cows, for horses, you know. So we took and cover up blankets and cover up. And we dragged it over to a shack, knocked off the safe-- knocked over and there was $36,000 Polish money. $36,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's happened, where you put it? Packages. Packages. So what do you do? So we take-- take strings. And we put in over here strings, both side.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took the strings and we put in just the money in the [INAUDIBLE]. This was military-- you know, you couldn't see too good, because it was hard. The uniforms were hard, so you couldn't see. And that's the way we smuggled out the money. Later, when he came back on the Russian vehicles, they were going to Stanislawow. So we all went on the-- on the-- on their trucks. And they took everybody, soldiers. They were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You go home, they say. I say, bah, how we going home? Just any-- any truck, any military truck will take you, whatever you want to go. And they took us to Stanislowow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we came to Stanislowow before, the city, you see thousands and thousands soldiers are sitting-- was a deep slide down from the road. Both sides, sitting soldiers here and soldiers on this side, and thousands of them. We say, oh, this is no good. Something what they're going to do, the soldiers. They let-- they don't let them go further on. I'm afraid that they're going to take him to the-- to the working battalion, they called-- you know, civilians, to work for the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I say, it's not good. You know what, fellas? Let's go start sneaking out. Let's go higher and higher. And there's houses, private houses, you know, Polish people, Ukrainian people, Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And let's start sneaking out. Then we can change our uniforms, throw the uniforms, take our civilian clothes, and forget about it. Nobody would know. And that's what we did. And we're going to meet. At 5 o'clock in the afternoon, we're going to meet by the city hall. We're going to meet by the city hall. And that's what's happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were five Jewish boys. And we were all together. So I sneaked out and I came to a house. I say, here, I got a nice uniform. It's a new, uniform, military. Maybe you got something to put on, civilian. Oh, yes. They took. Oh, boy, a uniform. They put on everything. I put it on. And so does the other guy did it. And that's the way we make up. And later, 5 o'clock, we all met there, 4:30, 5:00. We met there. And-- well, later on, we could go smaller station. But to Lemberg, I want to go through Lemberg to Jaworow to go there. You couldn't go. It was all the rails were all bombarded. And then the Russians had to bring in their own rails. The Russians are wider than the Polish. They have to make their rails, so we had to wait a week in Stanislowow 'til we can go to Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, we find a couple Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month still?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about September I think, September '49. The

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. September '39. So-- and we were-- so the two Jewish boys we found, they say maybe if you wanted, you can sleep by us. We have a big house. You could sleep by us. And they allowed the storekeepers to keep open. They told them, keep open selling whatever you used to sell with Polish zlotys. You can sell everything from the store. So we now bought new clothes, bought valises, bought new clothes, shoes, everything, everything from up to bottom. Money we had, so we put in packed up full valises with clothes. And we had a good time, you know. By Jews, we sleep there. We went out. Until after five days, I came to Lemberg. Other ones went in different directions. One of my friends is still someplace. I don't know. He's in New Jersey, Feiler. He is still in New Jersey. I met him in Bad Reichenhall and then I met him also once here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back?

speaker: interviewee
text: And to go back. So I came to Lemberg. And from Lemberg I went to Jaworow. And then I went to see my grandma was yet in [POLAND], and my uncles. By then, I didn't want to stay in the village, so I went to Jaworow. I had friends there, so I stayed in Jaworow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother and your brothers and your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in Krakow. They were in Krakow. They stayed in Krakow. But they sent out my both brothers. They send away before the Germans come, because they were-- first they were taken the boys, the men to work or hard labor camp or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother send away-- send away the two brothers of mine. And they came-- one came to a village in [POLAND], what he was working there by the Russians. And one brother was in Jaworow. He was a painter, so he also do painting with my wife's husband. He was a painter, because he learned by their parents to be a painter. So they were working together. In Jaworow?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother was in Jaworow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near what city is Jaworow, near what big city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jaworow is by Lemberg, by a big city, Lemberg. It was a county and there was a ghetto. There was a ghetto from-- starting with 3,000-- Jaworow, 3,000 Jews. And then with bringing in all the other Jews from the other villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that in a minute. Could you continue? So you arrived to Jaworow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I arrived to Jaworow. First I saw my-- my grandma and uncles. And then I went to the city. So when the Russians established themselves already in Jaworow-- so they-- I don't even remember how it happened, how I got this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they called me in. They called me in. Everybody wants to have a job, so I think I went and I reported that I'm here, this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I could stay here, because my papers was that I'm not Poland. I lived in Ukraine, you know, so I could stay here. So they gave me a job. They were building [NON-ENGLISH] in three cities. They were building [NON-ENGLISH] for the soldiers, also [NON-ENGLISH] and some [NON-ENGLISH]. I had all materials, the wood, the iron, everything what you need for building. They gave me to give out and [INAUDIBLE] whatever-- whatever I get it in, I wrote it down so much and so much. And the train was going there, the train, so they came in by train, all the materials, putting up on a big place, all closed in. And I had this. And I worked for months and months. Still we finished all the three buildings that we built, the [NON-ENGLISH] in the three cities. Now, a week before it was finished, they came, the First Secretary. He's the highest by the Russian. In every city there's a first-- a mayor and the first secretary. He has to do all intelligence. This was all intelligence, or Secret Service man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came over to me and he told me-- said, this is going to be closed already. It's all finished. So-- and we have a job for you, another job. I say, what is the job? he say, you'll take over the pilots magazine where they give out [INAUDIBLE], the food, the restaurant. And what I give out in the restaurant, what I give out all the food from a magazine for the pilots. They were the pilot. They had the best food. The pilots, they use to get the best food. By magazine what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A magazine, that means a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like a big storage house. Let's say a storage, storage, where you have all the groceries. You have everything there. You give out for the magazine. A storage house, that's a magazine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I say, what's happened to that fellow-- because I know the guy what he used to-- he worked there. I say, what's happened to the fella what he used to-- he works there? He said, he's not more there. I said, so-- so that's why we need-- we want to put you. I said, what's happened to him? He said, eh. He was a big crook. He stealed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an inspection came and he was missing 100 pounds of butter. He had liquor to give out. He had other things to give out. Missing a lot of stuff, the inspection, so he's a crook. So they gave him five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, he in jail? No, somehow he ran away. He ran away and he's not there. But he got-- they gave him five years jail. So I say, you want to give me a job like this? I should be responsible, because I know about the Russians. You have to be responsible. When they come, they check you out. They give you so much and so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you have too much, you didn't have time to steal to take it away. If you don't have enough-- if you have too much, you steal too much. You didn't have time to sell it or you have less, you know. You-- so anyway, I say, I cannot work in a my job like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry to interrupt you. And where are the $35,000 that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, later, we had-- we were going around to Lemberg. We were going visiting, you know. And we gave some-- the family in the city, they needed money, you know. And later on, the money didn't got so much worth, because they make the rubles. The ruble-- they changed to the rubles. So-- but meanwhile, we had enough money to keep us-- to keep us busy with the money. And so it happens, I say, I didn't want a job like this, I have to control. He said, let me tell you. The other guy, he was a crook. He didn't help us. I'm-- I have a big job in the city. But I need also to live. From the government, what the government pays us, we cannot live with a family. You have a family, a wife and children, you know. You cannot live with that. Then he would give us, you know, let's say meat every week, five pounds of meat, or give us fruit or give us liquor, you know. You can-- I say, how can you give and I'll give you and I have to give this one, I have to give this one. There's a lot of people to give in the city, the mayor, the-- the head from the-- from the restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So where I'm going to take to give? He say, you go. We'll tell you what to do and you'll be all right. We will go to the slaughter house. You'll bring him liquor. And you give him liquor. You give him coffee. You give him chocolate. He'll write it down 100 pounds of meat less than they slaughtered the cows. Nobody would know. He write it down so you can give us, you can give somebody else, and the rest you take for yourself and you can sell too. That's what they told me right in my eyes. You can sell to-- nobody lives in Russia without whatever we get from the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the war is going on. As a Jew, what do you feel? Now the war is going on, do you feel in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Russians were there. There was no war going on. This was sit still, you know, and you have a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware what was going on in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the rest of Europe, oh, I tell you, we know that where the Germans are, they were for the Jews was very bad. We know that. That was very bad. But for a while, there was no more wars, except Germany went further and further, took over Hungary and Romania and Estonia and so on and so on, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meanwhile, he say, if you are going to give us, support us, we know when inspection comes from a city, let's say from Lemberg, to inspect your magazine. And so it was another city's magazine. They go from one city to the other, inspect the magazines.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they let me know. I'm the [INAUDIBLE]. They let me know when they're coming. So I'm going to call you. I'm going to come over. And I'm going to tell you, make yourselves in order.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, you carry a book, how to-- you know. This, I got in today let's say 100 pound, 500 pounds I got today. I gave up 200 pounds. I still have 300 pound. [NON-ENGLISH] It was [INAUDIBLE]. Whatever you got, whatever you go out, and this you have to have. You put on the scale. I have three scales, I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. We can talk maybe a little bit further later about this. But how long did you have this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Til-- 'til the Germans came. Unexpectedly, they broke with the Russian, the pact. And not telling nothing, that we break our pact over there, but they broke the pact and they marched in to Jaworow, to all the cities. And they start taking over, taking over, taking over.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: What day? This was 1941. 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the month and the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wouldn't remember the month or the day. And then the Germans came over-- oh, Germans came over, was in July, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now we're going to take a pause to change tapes.
